ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Singaporean economic rice with fried meat accompaniments; 'rice dish' (FOODON_03315595) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Singaporean cai fan / economic rice; A00LC (rice-based composite dish) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps specifically to economic rice as a composite dish; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of economic rice with three meat dishes (two fried) typically weighs approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed white rice and 200 g of mixed meat accompaniments.

nutrient_source.title: Economic Rice (3 meats, 2 fried) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Economic Rice (3 meats, 2 fried), CR ID F05100044. All per-100g values are as reported. Fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data are not separately reported by HPB for this composite entry; those fields are set to -1 with full documentation in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice, steamed, as primary carbohydrate component
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 72 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for steamed white rice (GI ~72, high category), which constitutes the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (72 × 36.3) / 100 ≈ 26.1, using a 400 g serving with 19.01 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Composite dish GL estimated conservatively at 26.1 based on rice-dominant carbohydrate contribution. Input record GI confidence 0.591 noted; analogous estimate basis documented.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 329 mg/100 g with minimal dietary fibre (0.74 g/100 g), no vegetable component, and saturated fat from two fried meat portions contributing to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its borderline-high sodium content (329 mg/100 g), low fibre, and high saturated fat from deep-fried meats; if consumed, request steamed or braised meat substitutes, add a vegetable dish, and limit portion size to reduce total sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 72) white rice with 19.01 g/100 g carbohydrate, very low dietary fibre (0.74 g/100 g), and two fried meat portions creating a high glycemic load per serving (~26 GL units) that impairs postprandial glucose control
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or significantly reduce this meal due to its high GI, high glycemic load, and negligible fibre; substituting brown rice, replacing fried meats with steamed or braised lean protein, and adding non-starchy vegetables would substantially lower the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Two deep-fried meat portions contribute substantial saturated fat, trans fat risk, and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); combined with 329 mg/100 g sodium and absence of cardioprotective fibre, omega-3, or antioxidant-rich vegetables
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish in its standard form; the combination of deep-fried meats (elevated saturated fat and AGEs), high sodium, and absence of vegetables or whole grains creates a high cardiovascular risk profile — replacing fried meats with steamed fish or braised chicken and adding vegetables is strongly recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Two deep-fried meat items introduce substantial AGEs from high-heat cooking, strongly promoting joint inflammation; no anti-inflammatory components (omega-3, antioxidants, polyphenols, cruciferous vegetables) are present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish as the two fried meat portions generate pro-inflammatory AGEs and no positive anti-inflammatory dietary factors are present; replacing fried meats with steamed fish or braised chicken and adding at least two vegetable dishes is strongly advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Economic Rice (3 Meats, 2 Fried) — known locally in Singapore as 'cai fan' (菜饭 / 经济饭) — is a ubiquitous hawker-centre meal consisting of a generous portion of steamed white rice served alongside three meat or protein accompaniments, of which two are deep-fried and one is braised, stewed, or otherwise cooked without deep-frying. The dish is a staple of Singapore's hawker culture, offering an affordable, customisable, and filling meal popular for weekday lunches and dinners. Typical fried components include items such as deep-fried chicken wing, fried fish fillet, fried pork cutlet, or fried tofu, while the non-fried component may be braised pork belly, steamed egg, or stir-fried vegetables. The flavour profile is savoury and rich, with the fried items contributing a crispy exterior and umami depth, while the rice absorbs the sauces and gravies from the accompaniments. Nutritionally, this configuration is energy-dense at 219 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (10.8 g/100 g), relatively high fat (10.91 g/100 g) driven by the two fried components, and moderate carbohydrate (19.01 g/100 g) from white rice. Dietary fibre is low at 0.74 g/100 g and sodium is borderline-high at 329 mg/100 g. The glycemic index is high (~72) due to white rice dominance, producing a high glycemic load per standard serving (~26 GL units). Clinically, this meal is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without significant modification. The absence of vegetables and the presence of two fried meat portions make this one of the less healthful configurations of the economic rice format.
common_user_queries: How many calories in economic rice with 3 meats?
Is economic rice good for diabetics?
Hawker rice with fried chicken nutrition

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05100044) with confidence 0.95 as reported by the canonical source. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous value for steamed white rice (GI 72), which is the dominant carbohydrate source; this is a well-established and widely cited GI value. Multilingual names are standard Singaporean hawker terminology verified against common usage.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions (saturated, monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans), cholesterol, potassium, full micronutrient panel (vitamins A, B1, B2, B3, B6, B12, C, D, E, folate), phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this composite economic rice configuration are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F05100044 as of 2026; no alternative approved source (USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA) provides a sufficiently close composite match to substitute these values. Calcium (6.0 mg/100 g) and iron (0.18 mg/100 g) are reported by HPB and included. GI value of 72 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; the input record noted a GI confidence of 0.591, reflecting the composite nature of the dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated using a 400 g reference serving: total carbohydrate per serving = 76.04 g; GL estimated at 26.1 using the white-rice GI of 72 applied to the carbohydrate fraction. Halal status is not confirmed as fried meat types vary by stall; is_halal set to false by default. Allergen list (soy, gluten) is indicative based on typical economic rice preparations and may vary.
